author: @stevecummins324
text:
if using compressed gas anyway, could try  taper hydrostatic bearings, around the disk circumferance. (t's a way of 3D printing the bearings)
add bevels to the disk circumference.

then you need a pair of  housing rings with tapers to match the bevels exactly. radial holes so bolts can be used to clamp the disk in between them 
then add at least 3 pockets into the tapers to form gas bearing pads.  
add holes to allow pressure lines to be connected up to the bearing pads.
add air flow restrictors to the pressure lines. and connect them up to pressure source.

assemble the  housing rings to clamp the disk loosely at first. and increase the pressure. it will  leak badly at first. start spinning the turbine, and slowly increase the clamping, but always allow the disk to keep spinning. as it spins and gets tightened, the tapers will start to close up, and will wear themselves down into a near perfect fit. Akin to forming ground glass tapers as sometimes used for laboratory glasswear  
Once that done when the bearing is presssurised there's no solid to solid contact at all. zero wear and the only drag on rotation comes from air having tiny viscosity. the smaller the clearance the less gas flow there will be escaping the bearing. 
forces from pressure have a tendency to expand the housing rings. if the fit becomes close enough it's quite possible that when pressure released the housing rings will clamp down on the disk, and prevent it's rotation .but as soon as pressure applied again the clamping force will release.

replies.author: @stevecummins324
replies.text: Also... at high rpm, disks and flywheels tend to expand due to centrifugal forces. flyhweeels etc have a max rpm, beyond which the disk will literally fly apart. Between central bearing and periphery material is under radial tension. and those forces are usually the limiting factor for rpm.   With bearing on periphery material of the disk is under compression, and clearance will become even smaller if rpm rises. useful to prevent overspeeding. it's also much much easy to beef up a none rotating housing to handle greater forces than it is to do such to a flywheel. adding structure to a flywheel might increase it's strength, but doing such also increases the forces induced by spinning it..

author: @nirfz
text:
For the beginning: the V in the naming of those things was for "Vergeltung" =retaliation (or vengeance if you want to keep the correct first letter.)
And there are stories about why many of them experienced "navigation issues". Most were built using forced labour. And there often weren't "enough" guards.
So some forced labourers "tinkered" a bit with the navigation system.
(I remember hearing a former KZ inmate that had to work in such a factory telling that they pissed into/onto the delicate innertial navigation system occasionally before closing the lid.)

author: @RNMSC
text:
A tip on Tinker Cad, if you have a bunch of things you want to go around a circle, and they will be 'balanced' I.e. rotated 180 degrees, on the far side of the circle, use the duplicate, button to make a copy, then on the top of the right side, use the 'mirror' button to flip the freshly generated shape left to right. Now drag that shape to the far side of the circle, highlight all 3, then use the alligntool to align them "top to bottom' or 'left and right' across the center of the circle. Now select just the two shapes you want to copy around, and merge those. Now you make a copyof both, turn the copy 10 degrees, and then make another 16 copies to get them all around that ring. Finally I select all of them, align them left to right, and top to bottom, and make sure that they are properly centered on the center shape. 

The same technique for the magnets, though that may not need to be mirrored. If you trust the plastic more than the glue, you might use pockets oriented around the ring, just far enough in that you get a couple of perimeter rings outside of the pocket for the magnet. A drop of epoxy here should keep the magnet in place, but of course you're mostly on your own for orenting north and south poles on those magnets. I would think that the forces on the magnets from the disk spining would mostly be radial, rather than lateral, meaning that the glue wouldn't be under as much stress to hold onto the magents. On the other hand it moves the magnets away from the pickups. So either way.

As for the bearings, I'd suggest putting a 2mm "rim" in the middle of the disk to give the bearings a surface to seat against. That way if a bearing ends up being a little uneaven, it has a seat against which to allign, rather than potentially putting odd stress on the bearing and disk.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: cheers mate - I have done tutorials covering your suggestions but it great to have a refresher for folks who haven't seen those tutorials

replies.author: @RNMSC
replies.text:
@ThinkingandTinkering For me it's been part of making knitting looms, where the pegs need to stay oriented around a common center. And I've had the fun of working out angles, like how to get 7.5 degrees of rotation for a 48 pin loom, turns out if you turn it to something like 7 degrees, and you get to that 'counter quick enough, you can type in 7.4 (or -7.5 if you'v been rotating it the wrong way) and it will remember that change as well. I imagine that someone's going to need that for a 52 or 66 blade turbine some day. 

Who knows, I may have gleaned the rotation process from one of your own videos. In any case, was good to see, and I'm wondering if that blade profile is going to show up on a Magor Hardware fan showdown one of these days. That might be cool too.

Oh, another way to get that blade profile from a png or jpeg file is to open it in the Gimp, (photoshop may work too, I don't use it) use the magic wand to select the blade profile, then turn the selection into a path. Go voer to paths, and export the path as an SVG. I've had to do this for a couple of 3e prints where the image was needed, but not really available. There may be a way to do this directly in Inkscape too, I haven't delved far enough into that tool.

Almost always interesting to watch your videos. Have a great day!

author: @normandiebryant6989
text:
The blades you've used are for when the propellant (usually steam) is to expand and increase in speed as it passes through the rotating blades - a reaction turbine.  That bulge forms a narrowing of the space between the blades, like a nozzle, so the steam expands and accelerates as it leaves that narrowing (nozzle) and the curved tail of the blade redirects the flow to one side, the reaction forcing the blade and rotor to turn in the opposite direction.

However, what you seem to want is the propellant to increase in speed from stationary nozzles then transfer all the kinetic energy to blades - an impulse turbine.  You need slightly different shaped blades, like from a De Laval turbine, or half a Curtis Wheel.  Just google "impulse turbine blade cross section".

author: @adroitmobileware3992
text:
I seen many of your videos where you create something ie planetary gears, serpentine coils, etc. with standard dimensions for reuse in other projects. Might I suggest you do the same with your turbines so that you can stack them on a test generator with a standardized airflow source so that any time you create a new turbine you can test it and insert it into a stack ranked list to show how it compares to the other turbines you've built. Whenever I see you do a new one I always wonder if it out performs your others eg tesla, omni directional, blades with turbricles, etc.
Great work and video, thanks for all your inspiration

It seems that Global Geopolitical Stories are Going so far off from the Mainline of Logical Pathways that we are falling more and more into a 'Spiritual Resolution' to Actual World Problems...

What Politicians Needs..?
Good Scientists, Good Economists, Good Loyal Allies...or Inquisitors and Exorcists‽

Really they should ask themselves...

Because everything for Solving this Unsustainable Condition is Due..if it promote Humanity and Defy All Evil...

author: @AhmadKareem-x1r
text: Hello Mr. Robert Great video as usual... I always wonder about the balde for gas turbine. How can they withstand the huge torque when rotating the generator to produce electricity? With simple calculations, it appears that each megawatt requires more than three thousand Newton meters at   three thousand rpm per . Will the force resulting from compressing the air and then expanding it when mixed with fuel and igniting it be distributed on both sides of the blade? That is, the force embraces the blade .. I hope for clarification

replies.text: each blade doesn't experience that force mate - it experiences only a small share of the force which adds up

author: @jimmyjohnson7241
text:
A word of caution on your model:
Having built a  couple of turbines of similar size I would suggest that you reconsider the magnet holding geometry even for low power testing. 
 A single nozzle on that turbine even with tens of PSI pressure could result in many thousands of RPM.  The turbine itself in PLA should be fine, but I would not want to anywhere near the turbine disk loaded with magnets.  Even  if the glue joints hold up, the centrifugal loads on the disk with magnet notches will  likely exceed the disk material tensile strength.   Glass hard and brittle  NdFeB manet shards would be one of the last things I would want to have flying around  the workshop.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: stick an outer ring on it if you are worried mate - but if you have ever tried to separate super glue magnets and PLA - chances are you wouldn't worry
